title: Bayes & Base Rates
subtitle: Why a '99% accurate' positive can still mean you're fine
realWorldHook: A '99% accurate' test for a rare disease comes back positive. Most people — including many doctors — think you almost certainly have it. They're wrong, often badly.
conceptSummary: With a rare condition, the few true positives are swamped by false positives from the huge healthy group.

steps.title: The positive result that fools doctors
steps.body:
You take a test for a rare disease. It's '99% accurate.' It comes back positive.

Most people — including many *doctors* — think you almost certainly have it. They're wrong, often badly.

The same trap convicts innocent people in court and flags innocent travelers at airports. This lesson rewires how you read a positive result.

steps.prompt: A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. A test [[catches 99% of real cases|This is the test's *sensitivity* — the share of truly sick people it correctly flags as positive. Even at 99%, it can't overcome a very rare disease.]] and has a 5% [[false-positive rate|How often the test says 'positive' for someone who is actually healthy. A small rate across a huge healthy group still produces a flood of false alarms.]]. You test positive. What's the chance you actually have it?
steps.revealMessage: Lock it in. Now let's lay out 1,000 people and just count.

revealByOption.a: That's the answer almost everyone gives — and it's the trap this lesson is about. The test really is accurate, yet the truth is far lower. Let's count 1,000 people.
revealByOption.b: Lower than most people guess — but still way too high. Let's lay out 1,000 people and count the real positives against the false ones.
revealByOption.c: You're onto it — and it's even more extreme than most believe. Let's count it out.

steps.prompt: Here are 1,000 people. Drag the sliders for [[how common the disease is|Called the *prevalence* or base rate — the fraction of all people who actually have the condition before anyone is tested.]], how good the test is at catching it, and its false-positive rate. Watch how many positives are real versus false.
Of everyone who tests positive, only a small fraction actually have the disease. Why?

options.label: The handful of true positives is swamped by false positives from the huge healthy group
99% accuracy means 99% of positives are correct

feedback.correct: That's it. About 1 real case tests positive, but ~5% of the other 999 healthy people also test positive — that's ~50 false alarms drowning out the 1 true one.
feedback.incorrect: Count two groups separately: the tiny sick group and the enormous healthy group. A small error rate on a huge group produces a lot of false positives.

byOption.b: The test works exactly as advertised. The surprise comes from the base rate, not a faulty test.
byOption.c: Accuracy is about how the test treats sick and healthy people — not about your positives. With a rare disease, most positives still come from healthy people.

steps.hint: 5% of 999 healthy people is about 50 false positives. How does that compare to the ~1 true positive?

steps.title: The same count, written as a formula
steps.body: Counting the dots is [[Bayes' theorem|The rule for updating a probability after evidence: weigh how likely the evidence is when the hypothesis is true against how common that hypothesis was to begin with.]] in disguise. The chance a positive is real is the true positives over *all* positives — and because each group is its rate times how many people it covers, the enormous healthy group makes the false-positive term dominate the bottom of the fraction.

steps.prompt: Now slide the disease to be far more common — say 1 in 5. What happens to the chance a positive is real?

options.label: It rises sharply — more positives are now true
It stays the same; only the test matters

feedback.correct: Right. The base rate is half the story. Common disease → most positives are real. Rare disease → most positives are false. Same test, opposite conclusion.
feedback.incorrect: Try it on the array: make the disease common and the true-positive block grows much faster than the false-positive block.

byOption.b: The test never changed — yet the answer flips. That's the whole point: you can't read a positive without the base rate.

steps.hint: The test's accuracy didn't change — only how common the disease is. Watch the true-positive block grow.

steps.title: Always ask: how rare is it?
steps.body:
A test result is only half the story. The other half is the [[base rate|How common something is *before* any test — here 1 in 1,000. When the base rate is low, most positive results are false no matter how accurate the test.]] — how common the thing is *before* you tested.

The test is accurate; your conclusion still flips. Whenever someone quotes you a '99% accurate' result, your first question should be: how rare is what it's testing for?

Last lesson: the quiet number that runs every casino.
